# POLARIS — Locked Scope (Carney demo)

**Status:** LOCKED 2026-05-15. **Issue:** I-rdy-001 (#497), Phase 0A of the
Carney demo execution plan (`state/carney_demo_execution_plan_2026_05_15.md`).

**Purpose:** the single anti-drift source of truth for the Carney demo. The
LLM, the architecture, and the feature scope are frozen here. If a Codex
consult, an advisor, an external doc, or Claude's own reasoning ever proposes
something that contradicts §1, this document wins. Changes to §1 require an
explicit operator-signed reconciliation commit — not a silent edit.

**Pinning:** §1 (constraints) is LOCKED at this commit; its commit SHA is the
pin. §3 feature statuses are **PROVISIONAL** pending Phase 1 verification
(I-rdy-002, #498); the full document is re-pinned in Phase 0B (I-rdy-003,
#499) once statuses are verified.

---

## §1. Locked constraints — frozen, do not reopen

### §1.1 LLM
- **Generator: DeepSeek V4 Pro** — 1.6-trillion-parameter MoE (49B active/token), ships FP4 (experts) + FP8 (rest). Operator-locked, stated 6+ times.
- **Evaluator: Gemma 4 31B.**
- **Two-family invariant:** generator (DeepSeek lineage) and evaluator (Google/Gemma lineage) are different training lineages. `openrouter_client.check_family_segregation` enforces this at construction.
- **Concurrency: 1 concurrent session.** Traffic is operator-limited. This does not shrink the GPU requirement (the 1.6T model needs ~880 GB VRAM regardless of concurrency); it only bounds KV-cache headroom.
- Supersedes the prior `docs/hardware_decision.md` "Path C / V4 Flash" — that was overridden by operator directive to V4 Pro.

### §1.2 Hardware / sovereignty
- **Canadian sovereign GPU only.** V4 Pro requires 8× H200 or 12-16× H100 (Hopper-class — V100/A100 lack FP4/FP8 and cannot run it).
- **Not** OVH Canada (verified: zero GPU SKUs in any Canadian OVH region — all in France). **Not** France. **Not** any US company.
- Canadian sovereign GPU candidates: Vexxhost (Montréal), ISAIC (Edmonton) — both pending physical-location confirmation.
- Sovereignty threat model is NARROW: no runtime US LLM vendor calls + no report data in US jurisdiction. Model lineage, build toolchain, and DNS registrar are out of scope of the threat model.
- The orchestrator (CPU box) stays on OVH BHS5 — that is Canadian and acceptable; only the LLM inference must move to a Canadian GPU.

### §1.3 Architecture
- **The deployed v6 stack** is the architecture: `redis + api (FastAPI v6.2.0) + worker (Dramatiq) + webui (Next.js 16)` via `docker-compose.v6.yml`. **No rewrites.**
- Pipeline B (legacy LangGraph) and Pipeline C (frozen CLI) are **out of demo scope.**
- Frontend stack: Next.js 16 + React 19 + shadcn/ui + Tailwind 4.

### §1.4 Naming
- The legacy name **"BPEI" is banned** in all demo-facing code, docs, and config. Renamed to `ambiguity_detector`. Rename tracked by #434 (I-naming-001) + readiness Phase 2.

### §1.5 Demo
- Single venue, PM Mark Carney's office, flexible date in June 2026.
- Scope = the Carney plan (`docs/carney_delivery_plan_v6_2.md`). **No features beyond it.**

---

## §2. Canonical templates — the 8, frozen

The single source of truth is `config/scope_templates/`. Frontend cards,
frontend TypeScript types, and the v6 backend registry MUST all read from it
(reconciliation tracked by I-rdy-005, #501).

1. `clinical` — clinical drug audit
2. `policy` — public policy
3. `tech` — technology assessment
4. `due_diligence` — due diligence
5. `ai_sovereignty` — AI sovereignty
6. `canada_us` — Canada–US
7. `workforce` — workforce
8. `custom` — operator-defined catch-all

No template is added or removed without an operator-signed change to §2.

---

## §3. Feature scope — the 15 + cross-cutting capabilities

**Status values are PROVISIONAL** — set from the Codex gap analysis
(`state/carney_readiness_gaps_2026_05_15.md`), to be verified by Phase 1
(I-rdy-002, #498) and re-pinned in Phase 0B (I-rdy-003, #499).

**Binding rule:** *harness-page evidence and golden-fixture tests do NOT count
as feature-complete.* A feature is "complete" only when a live run exercises
it end-to-end in the deployed product.

### §3.1 The 15 user-visible features

| F# | Feature | Provisional status |
|---|---|---|
| F1 | Scope discovery + template browse | substrate exists; template registry mismatch |
| F2 | Ambiguity-detection disambiguation modal | substrate exists; not wired into main ask flow |
| F3 | Document upload + grounding | UI+API exist; async runs ignore uploaded docs |
| F4 | Live audit run (SSE reasoning visibility) | SSE substrate works; raw event cards, not frontier-grade |
| F5 | Report inspection / click-through audit | components exist; not on the live `/runs` report |
| F6 | Live citation hover overlay | components exist; harness/fixture-bound |
| F7 | Frame coverage panel | components exist; harness/fixture-bound |
| F8 | Contradiction navigation | components exist; harness/fixture-bound |
| F9 | Two-family disagreement signal | components exist; harness/fixture-bound |
| F10 | Inline visual generation (Vega-Lite) | spec builder/API/tests exist; fixture-bound |
| F11 | Report-scoped auditable follow-up | backend exists; disabled on the product run page |
| F12 | Side-by-side compare two reports | backend exists; no product route |
| F13 | Pin replay / "what changed" | page exists; demo-data-bound |
| F14 | Auditable research memory | page+API exist; in-memory demo store, not durable |
| F15 | Audit bundle export | tar.gz backend works; inspector JSON path fixture-only |

### §3.2 Cross-cutting capabilities (additional to F1-F15)

- Knowledge snowballing (`evidence_deepener.py`)
- Anti-sycophancy CI (paired-prompt, ELEPHANT methodology)
- Sovereignty enforcement (data classification + provider routing)
- Cross-jurisdiction synthesizer
- Strict-verify per-sentence provenance
- Two-family segregation invariant
- Evidence Contract Gate
- GPG-signed audit bundles

All marked PROVISIONAL pending Phase 1 verification.

## §5. Change protocol

- §1 (constraints) changes ONLY via an operator-signed reconciliation commit that explicitly states the prior value, the new value, and the reason.
- §2 / §3 / §4 changes require an operator-acknowledged commit.
- A Codex consult, advisor, or Claude reasoning step may *recommend* a change but may NOT enact one — §1 is operator-owned.
- This is the structural defense against the swing pattern (LLM drifting V4 Pro→Flash→Llama; provider drifting Canada→France) seen 2026-05-15.

**What this is:** the concrete execution path from where POLARIS is today to a
working Carney demo. It does NOT replace `docs/carney_delivery_plan_v6_2.md`
(the mission + F1-F15 spec). It closes the gap between "issues completed" and
"coherent demo-ready product." No new features.

---

## Locked constraints (do not reopen)

1. Generator = DeepSeek V4 Pro (1.6T MoE); Evaluator = Gemma 4 31B; 1 concurrent session, traffic limited.
2. Canadian sovereign GPU — 8× H200 / 12-16× H100. Not OVH-Canada (no Hopper there), not France, not US. Pursuing Vexxhost + ISAIC.
3. Architecture = deployed v6 stack (redis + api + worker + webui). No rewrites.
4. "BPEI" name banned — rename all 407 files to `ambiguity_detector`.
5. Demo: single venue, PM Carney's office, flexible June 2026 date.

## Honest framing of effort (Codex-corrected)

Two estimate bands, not one:
- **Optimistic:** Claude-side ready ~3.5 weeks; demo achievable second-half June IF the GPU lands early and scope stays locked to the demo path.
- **Conservative:** Claude-side ready ~4-5 weeks; demo late June / very-early July.
- v1's "3 weeks" was an over-claim. Plan against the conservative band; treat optimistic as best-case.

## The plan — phases + one parallel workstream

Legend: **[no GPU]** buildable/prevalidatable without the sovereign GPU (final sovereign gates still close after cutover). **[GPU]** needs the GPU. **[operator]** operator action.

- **Exit:** lock doc reflects grounded reality, fully pinned.

### Phase 2 — Deploy-reconciliation + rename **[no GPU]** — ~1-2 days

### Phase 3 — Fix the integration (P0 + the build-not-test P1s) **[no GPU]** — ~12-18 working days
The long pole. Each item a GitHub issue (§3.0 workflow). Codex-expanded scope:
1. **Auth/SSE operability** — real sign-in page; bearer-token injection in the web API client; SSE auth (token via query param or cookie — `EventSource` can't set headers).
2. **Templates → canonical 8** — one source of truth; landing page + TS types + v6 registry all read from `config/scope_templates/`.
3. **Model/config alignment** — purge stale V4 Flash / qwen / GLM; everything → V4 Pro + Gemma; transparency endpoint matches.
4. **Live-run artifact contract** — define the contract that maps a completed run's artifacts to what the inspector, charts, follow-up, compare, **pin replay**, **memory**, and bundle each consume. (Codex: this is the root fix.)
5. **Live-run → rich UI** — inspector, charts, follow-up, compare, **F13 pin replay**, **F14 memory**, bundle all accept a real completed run ID, not only golden fixtures.
6. **ambiguity_detector product wiring** — wire it into the main ask/create-run flow (rename alone does not make F2 product-integrated — Codex catch).
7. **Document grounding** — async worker passes uploaded `document_ids` into the pipeline; F3 works end-to-end.
8. **Cancellation/resume** — implement the disabled UI cancel + the xfailed backend resume (Codex catch — this is build work, not test work).
9. **Durable workspace-scoped memory** — F14 real durability + workspace isolation + cited recall + migration (Codex catch).
10. **1-concurrent-session enforcement** — queue/rejection UX when a second request arrives (Codex catch — required by the locked 1-concurrent constraint).
11. **Assemble the journey** — global nav in `layout.tsx`; coherent path land → template → ask → live audit → integrated report → inspect → follow-up/compare/pin/memory → signed bundle; hide the 17 harness routes.

# POLARIS Carney demo — readiness gap register

**Date:** 2026-05-15. **Source:** Claude grounded inventory + Codex independent
gap-analysis consult (`.codex/carney-readiness-gap-analysis/`).
**Codex verdict:** REQUEST_CHANGES — the readiness picture is missing material parts.

---

## The core finding (deeper than "test harness pages")

POLARIS's features were each built as **substrates + test-harness pages + golden
fixtures**, and each GitHub issue was completed *as scoped* and Codex-reviewed.
BUT the **rich product surfaces do not consume live run output** — the inspector,
charts, follow-up, compare, bundle paths are **fixture-bound** (they display
golden test fixtures, not the artifacts a real run produces).

Consequence: even with a working LLM, a real run will produce a run — but
click-through inspection, chart provenance, follow-up, compare, pin replay,
memory, and bundle export **from that same run** are not wired. This is the
`bpei_phantom_completion` pattern: issue-level "done" ≠ product-level "done."

So the GPU-wait window is NOT "testing time." It is **integration time** — and
that work mostly does not need the GPU.

---

## Per-feature honest status (Codex + Claude)

| Feature | Substrate | Product-integrated? |
|---|---|---|
| F1 scope/templates | landing UI exists | template cards don't match canonical templates |
| F2 ambiguity detector | backend + modal exist | not consistently wired into main run flow |
| F3 upload/grounding | upload UI + API exist | only text/markdown parse fully; async runs don't consume uploaded docs |
| F4 live audit SSE | Redis SSE substrate works | run page shows raw event cards, not frontier-grade |
| F5-F9 inspector/hover/coverage/contradiction/two-family | strong components exist | live in `/generation`, fixtures, harness pages — NOT the live `/runs/{id}` report |
| F10 charts | spec builder + API + tests | charts are fixture-bound; harness does the visual coverage |
| F11 follow-up | backend + harness exist | product run page has follow-up DISABLED |
| F12 compare | backend + harness exist | no real product route |
| F13 pin replay | page exists | uses demo data; says production fetch lands later |
| F14 memory | page + API exist | hardcoded demo workspace, in-memory store — not durable cited recall |
| F15 bundle export | tar.gz backend works for live artifacts | inspector JSON bundle path is fixture-only; clean-machine verify not proven |

## P0 — blocks the demo (8)

1. Secure Canadian H100/H200 GPU + full sovereign dress rehearsal. *(in progress — Vexxhost/ISAIC outreach sent)*
2. Make ONE coherent v6 product journey work end-to-end: landing → create run → live audit → integrated report → inspect claims → follow-up/compare/pin/memory → signed bundle.
3. Connect live run outputs to the rich inspector/report/bundle path (today they are fixture-indexed).
4. Fix auth for the deployed UI: sign-in page is a disabled placeholder, the web API client sends no bearer token, and browser `EventSource` can't send auth headers to protected SSE. **With auth enabled (as deployed), the UI cannot operate.**
5. Reconcile templates to the canonical 8 (`clinical, policy, tech, due_diligence, ai_sovereignty, canada_us, workforce, custom`) — currently split across 3-4 conflicting sources.
6. Align model/env/docs/transparency with locked **DeepSeek V4 Pro + Gemma 4 31B** — stale V4 Flash / qwen / GLM references remain.
7. Wire CLIENT document upload/grounding into async runs (the worker actor ignores uploaded `document_ids`).
8. Product-integrate F10-F15 instead of relying on harness/demo-data pages.

## P1 (7)

- Complete cancellation/resume (UI cancel disabled, backend xfailed).
- Make memory durable + workspace-scoped with cited recall.
- Run the full 22-type test matrix against the actual product journey (current coverage is mostly harness/fixture).
- Finish sovereignty + log-redaction CI and prove enforcement at every external call site.
- Signed-bundle clean-machine verification.
- Harden loading/empty/error/network/accessibility/performance states across the real journey.
- Demo logistics: fallback recordings, T-1 laptop drill, venue browser/network test, T1 source cache, PIPEDA/Law25 legal notice.

## P2 (4)

- Polish the visual design system + route consistency.
- Remove scaffold/demo-data copy from user-visible pages.
- Archive/hide the 17 harness pages from demo navigation.
- Clean stale docs/env comments after the lock doc is correct.

## Claude's filter on the Codex verdict

- The findings are **real and well-grounded** — consistent with the phantom-completion pattern and the per-issue-fixture build history.
- Many items are phrased "not evidenced / not proven" — that means *Codex could not find proof it works*, NOT *proven broken*. The P0 list needs a **verification pass first** (some may already work and just lack a product test) before assuming a full rebuild.
- The recommended sequence is sound and matches the operator's "lock scope first" instruction.

## Recommended sequence (Codex, accepted)

1. Write the **truthful lock document** — LLM + architecture + features + the canonical 8 templates + this RED gap list stated openly + the rule "harness/fixture evidence does not count as feature-complete."
2. Verify + fix P0 product connections, in order: auth/SSE → canonical templates → live-run→report/bundle → document grounding → locked model endpoint config.
3. Assemble the one navigable v6 journey; remove scaffold surfaces from the demo path.
4. OpenRouter V4 Pro rehearsal (non-confidential, key removed before demo).
5. Swap unchanged onto the Canadian GPU.
6. Full product-focused test matrix + clean-machine bundle verify + fallback drill + egress validation + venue rehearsal.

# Canada GPU — deep research for V4 Pro sovereign deployment

**Date:** 2026-05-15. **Question:** can V4 Pro run on OVH old GPU / where can we get Canadian GPU.
**Status:** research complete. One hard finding the operator must decide on.

---

## 1. What V4 Pro actually is (verified)

- **DeepSeek V4 Pro = 1.6 trillion total parameters**, Mixture-of-Experts, 49 billion active per token.
- Ships in **mixed precision**: MoE expert weights are **FP4**; the rest are **FP8**.
- 1M token context. Released 2026. MIT license. Weights on HuggingFace (`deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Pro`).
- **Memory to hold the model ≈ 880 GB** at its native FP4/FP8 precision.
- Source consensus: HuggingFace model card, Together AI, SiliconFlow, Modular, framia.pro all say 1.6T/49B. (One page — WaveSpeed — said "671B"; that is stale V3 data mislabelled. Even if it were 671B, the V100 conclusion below is unchanged.)

## 2. The hardware rule that decides everything

- FP4 and FP8 are **new number formats**. Only **NVIDIA Hopper (H100, H200)** and **Blackwell (B200)** GPUs can run them in hardware.
- **V100 (2017, "Volta")**: NO FP8, NO FP4, NO BF16, NO FlashAttention. It only does FP16/FP32.
- **A100 (2020, "Ampere")**: NO FP8, NO FP4. It does BF16/FP16/INT8.
- To run V4 Pro on a V100 or A100 you must **up-convert** the weights — FP4→FP16 makes them **4× bigger**, FP8→FP16 makes them **2× bigger**.

## 3. OVH old GPU (V100) — how many cards, honest answer

- OVH's Beauharnois (Quebec) datacentre has only **V100 / V100S** GPUs. No H100/H200/A100 in Canada (verified earlier via OVH API + 3rd-party).
- V4 Pro on V100 means up-converting everything to FP16: **880 GB native → roughly 3,200 GB in FP16**.
- V100 = 32 GB each. 3,200 GB ÷ 32 GB = **~100 V100 cards** just to hold the model. ~110-120 with working memory.
- **But the card count is the wrong question. It does not work at any count, because:**
  1. V100 has no FP8/FP4 hardware → forced 4× memory blow-up (above).
  2. V100 has no FlashAttention → V4 Pro's 1M-token attention runs catastrophically slow.
  3. OVH sells V100 only as small VMs (1, 2, or 4 GPUs each). 100 V100s = ~30 separate VMs with **no fast GPU-to-GPU link between them**. You cannot split a 1.6T model across 30 disconnected VMs.
  4. Even if somehow assembled, output speed would be a fraction of one word per second — a single report would take many hours.
- **Verdict: OVH old GPU is not "too expensive" — it is the wrong tool. A 2017 GPU cannot run a 2026 1.6-trillion-parameter FP4 model. Dead path. Do not pursue.**

## 4. What V4 Pro actually needs

- **8× H200** (141 GB each = 1,128 GB total) — the clean native config. Holds the model + room for context.
- OR **12-16× H100** (80 GB each) — works, tighter.
- **1 concurrent session does NOT reduce this.** The 880 GB is the model's own weight. Limiting traffic only saves a little context memory; it does not shrink the model. You still need 8× H200 (or 12-16× H100) minimum just to load V4 Pro.
- Cost, if Canadian H100 is found: at ISAIC's claimed $2.50 CAD/hr per H100, 16× H100 = $40/hr. A 5-day demo at ~15 hours uptime ≈ **$600 CAD**. Cost is NOT the blocker. **Availability is.**

## 5. ISAIC (Edmonton) — the honest picture

- ISAIC is a genuine Canadian non-profit, Edmonton, tied to University of Alberta. Sovereignty positioning real ("your data never leaves Canada").
- **Confirmed on their own site:** A100 80GB at **$1.63 CAD/hr**. A100 (Ampere) **cannot natively run V4 Pro** (no FP8/FP4) — would need ~40× A100, impractical.
- **They claim H100 at $2.50 CAD/hr.** H100 (Hopper) WOULD run V4 Pro.
- **CRITICAL CAVEAT (3rd-party April 2026 review):** ISAIC's listed H100s "may not be in Canada but rather in Gravelines, France." If ISAIC's H100 is physically in France, it FAILS the sovereignty requirement — same problem as OVH.
- **Action needed: contact ISAIC and ask one specific question — "Are your H100 GPUs physically in Edmonton, Alberta, or in France? We need Canadian-located H100."**

## 6. Vexxhost (Montréal) — the honest picture

- Genuine Canadian company, Montréal HQ, sovereign-cloud positioning.
- Public self-serve pricing page shows only old K80 GPUs. H100 is NOT self-serve — needs a sales conversation.
- We have portal credentials but the OpenStack API needs an Application Credential (operator must create it from the portal).
- **Action needed: contact Vexxhost sales — "Do you have 8× H200 or 16× H100, physically in Montréal, available June 5-9 2026?"**

## 7. Vast.ai — not a sovereign option

- Vast.ai owns no datacentres. It is a marketplace; hosts are independent individuals/companies.
- It has a country filter, so Canadian-located rigs may appear.
- But you cannot attest sovereignty: you do not know who legally owns the rig or what jurisdiction's laws apply. For a Prime-Minister-of-Canada demo this is not defensible.
- Earlier POLARIS planning correctly treated Vast as a US dev cluster only, never the sovereign path.

## 8. US companies with Canadian regions (listed for honesty — all FAIL sovereignty)

- DigitalOcean Toronto: has H100 (~$2.99/hr USD) but US company; reported sold out April 2026.
- AWS ca-central-1: H100/A100 on the sheet, US company, zero default quota, approval routes through US.
- Google Cloud Montreal/Toronto: only old or inference-only GPU, US company.
- Azure Canada Central: some GPU, US company.

## 9. The honest collision the operator must resolve

Two locked constraints **may not have a clean intersection**:
- **V4 Pro (locked)** → needs Hopper H100/H200.
- **Canadian sovereign (locked)** → Canada's H100/H200 supply is genuinely thin.

The only two candidate Canadian H100 sources both need verification:
- **ISAIC H100** — must confirm it is physically in Edmonton, not France.
- **Vexxhost H100/H200** — must confirm availability + Montréal location via sales.

If BOTH turn out unavailable in Canada, that is a genuine halt-and-decide for the operator (not Claude): accept non-Canadian H100, or change model, or move the date. Claude does not choose — Claude reports.

## 10. Recommended immediate actions (operator + Claude split)

- **Operator:** contact ISAIC (info@isaic.ca) — ask the §5 question. Contact Vexxhost sales — ask the §6 question.
- **Claude:** can draft both emails now. Can also pre-stage everything provider-agnostic (vLLM config for V4 Pro, generator/evaluator URL separation) so the day a Canadian H100 is confirmed, deployment is fast.
- **Do NOT pursue OVH V100.** It is a dead path — §3.

.\docs\v1_1_backlog.md:26:The generator drafts ~6000 words, strict_verify drops ~60-70%, leaving 2346.
.\docs\v1_1_backlog.md:30:1. **~~Raise `section_max_tokens` 2400 в†’ 4800.~~** **EMPIRICALLY INVALIDATED 2026-04-30.** Tested via `PG_SECTION_MAX_TOKENS=4800` env override. Result: word count went from 2346 в†’ 2032 (REGRESSION, not improvement). Hypothesis: larger generation budget produces more low-quality tail sentences that fail strict_verify; additional retries actually shrink the final kept prose. The token cap is NOT the bottleneck вЂ” it's the draft quality crossing the 0.4 kept_fraction floor.
.\docs\v1_1_backlog.md:32:2. **Add 1-2 more sections to outline.** Current outline has 3 sections; competitors have 8-15 narrative blocks. Mostly a synthesizer prompt change. **Risk:** more sections = more strict_verify failures = more retries.
.\docs\v1_1_backlog.md:34:3. **~~Raise the kept_fraction floor 0.4 в†’ 0.35.~~** **EMPIRICALLY INVALIDATED 2026-04-30.** Tested via `PG_MIN_KEPT_FRACTION=0.35`. Result: 2346 в†’ 2285 (-2.6% regression). Confirms the bottleneck is structural вЂ” synthesizer produces shorter prose because the strict_verify-passing CONTENT pool is bounded by the evidence pool, not by retry threshold. Both retry-knob options (1 + 3) now invalidated.
.\docs\v1_1_backlog.md:58:Take the deterministic "Field1: value [N]. .\docs\v1_1_backlog.md:190:**Decision:** v1.0 ships at 4/7. The 2 BEHIND-BOTH gaps are synthesizer capacity issues, NOT POLARIS having lower-quality evidence (POLARIS has MORE citations, MORE regulatory coverage, MORE jurisdictional precision). .\docs\transparency.md:61:- `api.semanticscholar.org` вЂ” Semantic Scholar (US AI2 non-profit; disclosed; removable for stricter sovereignty)
.\docs\transparency.md:72:The rationale for accepting the exception: POLARIS's sovereignty constraint protects the **LLM inference path and the generated report data** вЂ” those run on Canadian/non-US infrastructure (Vexxhost orchestrator + OVH H200 inference). A web-search query is a short keyword string carrying no confidential research content; the sensitive artifact is the synthesized report, which never transits Serper. A reviewer who wants zero US touch in the search path can swap Serper for a non-US provider (Mojeek UK / Qwant FR / Ecosia DE) вЂ” the retrieval code is provider-shaped вЂ” but that is not required for the sovereignty posture as scoped.
.\docs\transparency.md:76:**No AWS endpoints.** SSM, EC2 messages, and S3 hosts are explicitly NOT in the allowlist (per I-carney-008 sovereignty audit).
.\docs\transparency.md:78:**Tier-1 evidence fetching under lockdown.** Pipeline-A's sovereignty filter accepts only T1 (regulatory + clinical guideline) sources by default. The allowlist covers those T1 corpus hosts + the bib/DOI resolution infrastructure needed to attach them to cites. Arbitrary publisher domains (nature.com, nejm.org, thelancet.com, etc.) are NOT in the allowlist вЂ” they're T2/T3 and excluded by the sovereignty filter anyway; the egress drop is defense-in-depth.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_strategic_review\codex_stdout.log:2735:outputs\codex_findings\v30_m66_plan_review\pass3_codex_stdout.log:5035:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:134:On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.

Blocker 3 (SectionResult parity): RESOLVED вЂ” `run_contract_section()` now builds body-only `raw_draft`, runs rewrite в†’ `strict_verify` в†’ `resolve_provenance_to_citations`, then re-groups kept sentences by slot and re-emits `### subsection_title` blocks with `[N]` markers plus populated `biblio_slice` at `src/polaris_graph/generator/contract_section_runner.py:250`. .\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_strategic_review\codex_stdout.log:3136:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:80:- "`strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_strategic_review\codex_stdout.log:3137:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:97:   to be from a licensed source. strict_verify passes because it
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_strategic_review\codex_stdout.log:3138:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:123:The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_strategic_review\codex_stdout.log:3139:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:134:On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_strategic_review\codex_stdout.log:11820:The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_strategic_review\codex_stdout.log:11831:On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:3844:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:80:- "`strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:3845:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:97:   to be from a licensed source. strict_verify passes because it
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:3846:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:123:The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:3847:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:134:On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:4668:outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7804:+The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:4669:outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7815:+On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.

Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:4672:outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7851:+On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:4673:outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7863:+3. Keep `strict_verify` intact.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:4674:outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7871:Position taken: the real root cause is absence of a mandatory clinical report contract. POLARIS is still corpus-led, so V29 only improved evidence transport inside the wrong architecture. I also took a hard line that `strict_verify` is not the wall, licensed/full-text access is a real ceiling, frame-first should lift the stuck dimensions, but 7/7 is not guaranteed without solving evidence access.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:4675:outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7882:+The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:4711:outputs\codex_findings\v29_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:116:The strict_verify gate is both the reason POLARIS is credible and the reason cheap imitation of competitor prose fails. Competitors can quote or synthesize trial numbers from memory or inaccessible primary papers. POLARIS must match claims to fetched direct_quote text. For paywalled NEJM/Lancet primaries with thin fetched text, strict_verify blocks the exact numeric specificity that wins clinical dimensions. The answer is not to discard strict_verify globally. The answer is to make primary publication reachability and quote adequacy a first-class pipeline contract before generation begins.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:4712:outputs\codex_findings\v29_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:128:I would not choose gamma as the main path. Relaxing strict_verify for narrative sections may help Gemini-style mechanism prose, but it attacks the wrong bottleneck and risks losing POLARIS's strongest brand: traceable claims. A narrower version is acceptable later: strict numeric verification for all numeric claims, with a separate "synthesis-only" mode for nonnumeric interpretation that still requires same-paragraph evidence support. That is a refinement, not the core roadmap.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:5130:outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\_codex_stdout.txt:110:### Strategy Оі: content-quality overhaul at strict_verify (2-3 cycles)
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:5131:outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\_codex_stdout.txt:112:Relax strict_verify for narrative-depth sections (Mechanism,
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:5135:outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\_codex_stdout.txt:866:+The strict_verify gate is both the reason POLARIS is credible and the reason cheap imitation of competitor prose fails. Competitors can quote or synthesize trial numbers from memory or inaccessible primary papers. POLARIS must match claims to fetched direct_quote text. For paywalled NEJM/Lancet primaries with thin fetched text, strict_verify blocks the exact numeric specificity that wins clinical dimensions. The answer is not to discard strict_verify globally. The answer is to make primary publication reachability and quote adequacy a first-class pipeline contract before generation begins.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:5136:outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\_codex_stdout.txt:878:+I would not choose gamma as the main path. Relaxing strict_verify for narrative sections may help Gemini-style mechanism prose, but it attacks the wrong bottleneck and risks losing POLARIS's strongest brand: traceable claims. A narrower version is acceptable later: strict numeric verification for all numeric claims, with a separate "synthesis-only" mode for nonnumeric interpretation that still requires same-paragraph evidence support. That is a refinement, not the core roadmap.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_phase2_run12_audit\codex_stdout.log:5156:outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\_codex_stdout.txt:1242:+The strict_verify gate is both the reason POLARIS is credible and the reason cheap imitation of competitor prose fails. .\outputs\codex_findings\v30_m66_plan_review\pass3_codex_stdout.log:5035:outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:134:On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_m66_plan_review\codex_stdout.log:15050:6. Body prose format / sentence splitter: The Title Case trick is not Unicode-robust. `render_slot_prose()` only uppercases the first codepoint, while `_SENTENCE_SPLIT_RE` only recognizes ASCII `[A-Z]`, so non-ASCII-leading labels are not guaranteed sentence boundaries. A local smoke run on the current code also showed overlap failures: the heading+`N` sentence and the `Population` / `Comparator` / `Timepoint` sentences were dropped by `strict_verify`.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_m66_plan_review\codex_stdout.log:15066:- Blocker: `run_contract_section()` does not produce legacy-compatible section output. It concatenates headings into sentence text, runs `strict_verify()`, then returns raw `[#ev:...]` prose with `biblio_slice=[]` instead of resolving numbered citations. Downstream M-44/global bibliography/remap/report assembly all assume the legacy `[N]` + `biblio_slice` shape. `src/polaris_graph/generator/contract_section_runner.py:240-315`, `src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:1910-1968`, `src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:3235-3244`, `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1196-1226`, `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py:1510-1520`
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_m66_plan_review\codex_stdout.log:15098:3. Blocker 3 (SectionResult parity): RESOLVED вЂ” `run_contract_section()` now builds body-only `raw_draft`, runs rewrite в†’ `strict_verify` в†’ `resolve_provenance_to_citations`, then re-groups kept sentences by slot and re-emits `### subsection_title` blocks with `[N]` markers plus populated `biblio_slice` at `src/polaris_graph/generator/contract_section_runner.py:250`. The re-grouping logic mirrors `resolve_provenance_to_citations()`'s acceptance filters (`>=3` content words, `>=15` chars, whitespace cleanup), citation numbering stays aligned with the flat `biblio_slice`, and the fallback path uses `resolved_body`, so `[N]` markers plus bibliography survive even when grouping fails. .\outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:80:- "`strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:97:   to be from a licensed source. strict_verify passes because it
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:123:The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v30_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:134:On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7804:+The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7815:+On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7882:+The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. So the real failure is: no frame, no slot obligation, no bind between slot and source, and verification that is purely retrospective. In that design, primaries will always be fragile. Retrieval misses them, selection can displace them, and even when injected, the model can ignore them by writing around them. V29 proved that.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7893:+On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7905:+3. Keep `strict_verify` intact.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\_codex_stdout.txt:7914:Position taken: the real root cause is absence of a mandatory clinical report contract. POLARIS is still corpus-led, so V29 only improved evidence transport inside the wrong architecture. I also took a hard line that `strict_verify` is not the wall, licensed/full-text access is a real ceiling, frame-first should lift the stuck dimensions, but 7/7 is not guaranteed without solving evidence access.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\findings.md:3:The code path supports that reading. The outline planner explicitly says "if the evidence doesn't support a topic, don't include it." The section generator writes from an evidence subset, not from a required editorial frame. `strict_verify` then acts only on what was written. .\outputs\codex_findings\v29_true_root_cause\findings.md:14:On `strict_verify`: it is not the root cause, and I would not relax it. `strict_verify` is doing what it should do: refusing to bless claims that are not grounded in retrieved text. The problem is upstream. Today, if a frame element is not retrievable in machine-readable form, the element silently disappears because nothing in the pipeline reserves an empty slot and emits an explicit coverage failure. In a frame-first design, `strict_verify` remains strict, but the output contract changes from omission to explicit insufficiency: "SURPASS-2 primary publication not retrievable from accessible sources; subsection limited to metadata-level reporting." That is honest and structurally stable. The current architecture converts retrieval failure into content absence. That is the real bug.

.\outputs\audits\v32_baseline_content_audit\CROSS_REVIEW.md:124:**Faithfulness verdict**: the report is mostly grounded but has localized fabrication patterns the strict_verify gate missed:
.\outputs\audits\v32_baseline_content_audit\CROSS_REVIEW.md:129:The strict_verify gate's `[#ev:ev_id:start-end]` provenance check verifies that a citation marker exists, but does NOT verify that the claim's specific facts are in the cited span вЂ” it only checks numeric/word overlap heuristics. **The architectural gap**: provenance presence в‰  provenance correctness. A serious quality fix would be a content-level verification pass (e.g. NLI entailment check) on top of the existing token-presence check.
.\outputs\audits\v32_baseline_content_audit\CROSS_REVIEW.md:131:This is the honest state of the report. It is NOT 5-BEAT-BOTH publishable quality вЂ” it has 1 fabricated mechanistic claim, 2 unverifiable specificity claims, and several minor inflations. The architectural infrastructure (I-bug-088/089/090) is sound; the **provenance-correctness gap** at the strict_verify layer is the next real architectural piece of work.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:116:The strict_verify gate is both the reason POLARIS is credible and the reason cheap imitation of competitor prose fails. Competitors can quote or synthesize trial numbers from memory or inaccessible primary papers. POLARIS must match claims to fetched direct_quote text. For paywalled NEJM/Lancet primaries with thin fetched text, strict_verify blocks the exact numeric specificity that wins clinical dimensions. The answer is not to discard strict_verify globally. The answer is to make primary publication reachability and quote adequacy a first-class pipeline contract before generation begins.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v29_fix_plan_review_pass1\_codex_stdout.txt:128:I would not choose gamma as the main path. A narrower version is acceptable later: strict numeric verification for all numeric claims, with a separate "synthesis-only" mode for nonnumeric interpretation that still requires same-paragraph evidence support. That is a refinement, not the core roadmap.

.\outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\_codex_stdout.txt:866:+The strict_verify gate is both the reason POLARIS is credible and the reason cheap imitation of competitor prose fails. Competitors can quote or synthesize trial numbers from memory or inaccessible primary papers. POLARIS must match claims to fetched direct_quote text. For paywalled NEJM/Lancet primaries with thin fetched text, strict_verify blocks the exact numeric specificity that wins clinical dimensions. The answer is not to discard strict_verify globally. The answer is to make primary publication reachability and quote adequacy a first-class pipeline contract before generation begins.
.\outputs\codex_findings\v28_strategic_path\_codex_stdout.txt:878:+I would not choose gamma as the main path. Relaxing strict_verify for narrative sections may help Gemini-style mechanism prose, but it attacks the wrong bottleneck and risks losing POLARIS's strongest brand: traceable claims. A narrower version is acceptable later: strict numeric verification for all numeric claims, with a separate "synthesis-only" mode for nonnumeric interpretation that still requires same-paragraph evidence support. That is a refinement, not the core roadmap.

state\compare_gemini_q5.md:63:The introduction of Bill C-64, the Pharmacare Act, in February 2024 represents the federal government's most significant legislative attempt to rectify this historical gap. Passing its third reading in the House of Commons in June 2024, the legislation establishes the foundational framework for the initial stage of a national universal pharmacare program. The Act proposes a collaborative model wherein the federal government will work alongside provincial and territorial jurisdictions to provide universal, single-payer, first-dollar coverage for specific therapeutic categories, beginning primarily with medications for diabetes and contraception.   
state\compare_gemini_q5.md:65:However, the impending rollout of this legislation has catalyzed intense scrutiny and fierce policy debate regarding the optimal structural design of a national formulary. Policymakers face a critical, path-dependent choice: Should Canada adopt a "fill-the-gaps" model that preserves the lucrative existing private insurance market, or should it transition toward a comprehensive, single-payer, actively managed system? To rigorously evaluate the potential efficacy and trajectory of the federal Pharmacare Act, it is imperative to conduct an exhaustive comparative analysis of the evidence base from international jurisdictions that have navigated these precise structural dilemmas.   
state\compare_gemini_q5.md:87:The introduction of the federal Pharmacare Act seeks to directly disrupt this cycle of deterioration by eliminating the point-of-care financial barriers to highly effective, routine medications. By specifically targeting diabetes medications in its initial rollout phase—a condition affecting roughly 30,000 school-aged children with type 1 diabetes and millions of adults across the nation—the legislation aims to prevent the rationing of life-saving therapies such as insulin, metformin, Sulfonylureas, and SGLT-2 inhibitors. Ensuring first-dollar coverage for these agents is intended to avert severe downstream complications, including diabetic ketoacidosis, progressive neuropathy, and major cardiovascular events. Furthermore, the Act establishes a dedicated fund to support access to diabetes devices and supplies, attempting to harmonize the currently inconsistent level of coverage provided across different provincial jurisdictions.

I will be synthesizing findings from various academic and governmental sources to determine what lessons can be applied to the phased implementation of a national pharmacare strategy.

I am evaluating how different jurisdictions use unit-cost monitoring and bulk-purchasing to manage this gap, particularly as they transition to newer voluntary pricing schemes designed to incentivize innovation while maintaining fiscal sustainability.
state\compare_gemini_q4.md:56:The Canadian housing market between 2020 and 2026 experienced an era of profound structural volatility, characterized by unprecedented macroeconomic shocks, severe demographic fluctuations, and an aggressive, multi-jurisdictional policy response. As the national homebuying affordability ratio deteriorated significantly—rising from 39% in 2019 to 54% in 2024—and the national average home price stabilized near $653,000 in early 2026, the political and economic imperative to resolve the housing crisis reached a critical zenith. The fundamental diagnosis of the Canadian housing market is universally acknowledged among economists and policymakers: a severe, deeply entrenched deficit of structural supply relative to population-driven demand. To restore affordability metrics to their 2019 baseline, the Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) estimates that between 430,000 and 480,000 housing starts are required annually through 2035. This target remains drastically above the 259,000 housing units started nationally in 2025, a figure that, while representing a 6% year-over-year increase, still falls critically short of systemic requirements.   
state\compare_gemini_q4.md:88:The immediate statistical evaluation of the HAF reveals a stark dichotomy between aggregate national success and severe regional failure. An independent July 2025 assessment by the Parliamentary Budget Officer (PBO) demonstrated that, between October 2024 and April 2025, participating CMAs experienced a 31% increase in permit issuances and a 5% increase in actual housing starts compared to their 2018–2023 seasonally adjusted monthly averages. This performance was particularly notable when contrasted with non-participating jurisdictions, which suffered a 15% decline in permits and a 28% drop in housing starts over the same period.   
state\compare_gemini_q4.md:148:This federal prohibition operates in tandem with aggressive taxation models deployed by provincial and municipal authorities. For instance, the federal government introduced the Underused Housing Tax in 2022, assessing a 1% annual value levy strictly on non-primary residences owned by foreign nationals. Concurrently, British Columbia significantly expanded its Speculation and Vacancy Tax, targeting housing in metropolitan areas; the tax rate for property owners who are foreign nationals or who earn the majority of their income overseas was escalated to 2%, with a further increase to 3% mandated for 2026. Similar speculative levies have been adopted across multiple jurisdictions in Ontario.   
state\compare_gemini_q4.md:190:Furthermore, the demographic brake devastated the secondary rental market, which consists primarily of investor-owned condominiums. Deprived of the international student and temporary worker cohorts that historically occupied these units, asking rents for condos plummeted fastest in the jurisdictions most exposed to immigration changes: dropping 7.4% in Ontario and a staggering 11.6% in British Columbia. While unequivocally effective at suppressing demand-side inflation, this demographic pacing precipitated a profound structural paradox that threatens the long-term viability of the Canadian housing supply chain.

Driven by economic uncertainty, budgetary constraints, and rising citizen expectations for seamless digital service delivery, governments are increasingly turning to AI to modernize operations. However, public sector knowledge workers are significantly more exposed to AI applications than the broader Canadian labour force (74% versus 56%).
